Maths - Measurement and data.skill: Area of composite figures
Maths - Measurement and data.prompt: This topic covers area of composite figures for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.9(C)). Students decompose irregular shapes into familiar shapes and sum or subtract their areas. Key concepts include: identifying component shapes, calculating each area, determining add vs. subtract, and handling circular parts. Vocabulary: composite figure, decompose, area, rectangle, triangle, semicircle. Common misconceptions: not identifying all components and confusing add vs. subtract. Practice should include floor plans and design problems appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Measurement and data.skill: Comparing data using box plots and dot plots
Maths - Measurement and data.prompt: This topic covers comparing data using box plots and dot plots for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.12(A)). Students analyze and compare data sets displayed as box plots or dot plots. Key concepts include: five-number summary (min, Q1, median, Q3, max), clusters/gaps/outliers in dot plots, and comparing center, spread, and shape. Vocabulary: box plot, dot plot, median, quartile, range, interquartile range, outlier, distribution. Common misconceptions: misreading quartiles and confusing frequency with value. Practice should include side-by-side comparisons appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Measurement and data.skill: Inference from random samples
Maths - Measurement and data.prompt: This topic covers making inferences from random samples for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.12(B)). Students use sample data to draw inferences about populations. Key concepts include: representative samples, using sample statistics to estimate population parameters, sampling variability, and sample size effects. Vocabulary: random sample, population, inference, representative, sample size, estimate, parameter, statistic. Common misconceptions: thinking one sample perfectly represents the population and confusing biased with random samples. Practice should include survey scenarios appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Measurement and data.skill: Comparing populations based on random samples
Maths - Measurement and data.prompt: This topic covers comparing populations based on random samples for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.12(C)). Students compare two populations by analyzing random samples. Key concepts include: using measures of center and variability to compare, making informal inferences, and understanding distribution overlap. Vocabulary: population, random sample, compare, mean, median, variability, range, MAD, IQR, overlap. Common misconceptions: comparing only centers without variability and drawing conclusions from non-random samples. Practice should include comparative analysis appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Measurement and data.skill: Measures of central tendency and variability
Maths - Measurement and data.prompt: This topic covers measures of central tendency and variability for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.12(A)). Students calculate and interpret mean, median, mode, range, and other measures. Key concepts include: mean as balance point, median as middle value, mode as most frequent, range, choosing appropriate measures, and outlier effects. Vocabulary: mean, median, mode, range, central tendency, variability, outlier, distribution. Common misconceptions: always using mean, not ordering data for median, and thinking range measures center. Practice should include data analysis appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Measurement and data.skill: Volume of cubes and prisms
Maths - Measurement and data.prompt: This topic covers volume of cubes and rectangular prisms for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.9(A)). Students calculate volume using V = lwh for rectangular prisms and V = s^3 for cubes. Key concepts include: volume as the number of unit cubes that fill a space, the relationship between a cube (special prism) and general rectangular prism, and using appropriate formulas. Vocabulary: volume, cube, rectangular prism, length, width, height, edge, cubic units. Common misconceptions: confusing area and volume formulas and not cubing the edge for cubes. Practice should include computation and real-world contexts appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Measurement and data.skill: Volume of cubes and rectangular prisms: word problems
Maths - Measurement and data.prompt: This topic covers word problems involving volume of cubes and rectangular prisms for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.9(A)). Students apply volume formulas to solve real-world problems. Key contexts include: filling containers, packing boxes, aquariums, and room dimensions. Key concepts include: identifying dimensions from context, choosing the correct formula, computing, and interpreting units. Vocabulary: volume, cube, rectangular prism, length, width, height, cubic units, capacity. Common misconceptions: using wrong dimensions, confusing surface area with volume, and not using cubic units. Practice should include practical scenarios appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Measurement and data.skill: Volume of pyramids
Maths - Measurement and data.prompt: This topic covers volume of pyramids for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.9(A)). Students calculate pyramid volume using V = (1/3)Bh where B is the base area and h is the height. Key concepts include: identifying the base shape, calculating base area, using the perpendicular height (not slant height), and applying the 1/3 factor. Vocabulary: pyramid, volume, base area, height, slant height, one-third, cubic units. Common misconceptions: forgetting the 1/3 factor, using slant height instead of perpendicular height, and errors calculating the base area for non-square bases. Practice should include various pyramid types appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Measurement and data.skill: Parts of a circle
Maths - Measurement and data.prompt: This topic covers parts of a circle for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.8(C)). Students identify and describe the key components of a circle. Key parts include: center, radius, diameter, chord, arc, circumference, semicircle, and sector. Key relationships: diameter = 2 x radius, circumference = pi x diameter. Vocabulary: center, radius, diameter, chord, arc, circumference, semicircle, sector, tangent. Common misconceptions: confusing radius and diameter, thinking a chord must pass through the center, and not understanding that diameter is a special chord. Practice should include identification and labeling activities appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Measurement and data.skill: Circumference of circles
Maths - Measurement and data.prompt: This topic covers circumference of circles for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.9(B)). Students calculate the distance around a circle using C = pi*d or C = 2*pi*r. Key concepts include: understanding circumference as the perimeter of a circle, the relationship between radius and diameter, using pi (~3.14 or 22/7), and solving for circumference, radius, or diameter. Vocabulary: circumference, circle, radius, diameter, pi, perimeter. Common misconceptions: confusing circumference with area and using radius when diameter is given (or vice versa). Practice should include computation and real-world problems appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Measurement and data.skill: Area of circles
Maths - Measurement and data.prompt: This topic covers area of circles for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.9(B)). Students calculate the area enclosed by a circle using A = pi*r^2. Key concepts include: understanding why the radius is squared, estimating area before computing, solving for area given radius or diameter, and solving for radius given area. Vocabulary: area, circle, radius, diameter, pi, squared, square units. Common misconceptions: forgetting to square the radius, using diameter instead of radius, and confusing area with circumference. Practice should include computation and real-world problems appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Measurement and data.skill: Circles: word problems
Maths - Measurement and data.prompt: This topic covers circle word problems for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.9(B)). Students apply circumference and area formulas to solve real-world problems involving circular shapes. Key contexts include: gardens, pizza, wheels, pools, and tracks. Key concepts include: choosing between circumference and area, extracting radius or diameter from context, computing, and interpreting results. Vocabulary: circle, circumference, area, radius, diameter, pi, real-world. Common misconceptions: choosing the wrong formula for the situation and not halving the diameter to get the radius. Practice should include practical applications appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Measurement and data.skill: Semicircles: calculate area, perimeter, radius, and diameter
Maths - Measurement and data.prompt: This topic covers semicircle calculations for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.9(B)). Students calculate area, perimeter, radius, and diameter of semicircles. Key concepts include: area of a semicircle = (1/2)*pi*r^2, perimeter includes the curved part (pi*r) plus the diameter, and the relationship between semicircle and full circle measurements. Vocabulary: semicircle, area, perimeter, radius, diameter, pi, half circle, curved part, straight edge. Common misconceptions: forgetting to include the straight edge in perimeter, not halving the area formula, and confusing curved perimeter with full circumference. Practice should include computation and real-world problems appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Measurement and data.skill: Quarter circles: calculate area, perimeter, and radius
Maths - Measurement and data.prompt: This topic covers quarter circle calculations for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.9(B)). Students calculate area, perimeter, and radius of quarter circles. Key concepts include: area = (1/4)*pi*r^2, perimeter includes the curved part (pi*r/2) plus two radii, and the relationship between quarter circle and full circle measurements. Vocabulary: quarter circle, area, perimeter, radius, pi, one-fourth, arc length, right angle. Common misconceptions: forgetting to include both straight edges in perimeter, not dividing by 4 for area, and confusing with semicircle formulas. Practice should include computation appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Measurement and data.skill: Area of compound figures with triangles
Maths - Measurement and data.prompt: This topic covers area of compound figures with triangles for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.9(C)). Students decompose complex shapes into rectangles and triangles to find total area. Key concepts include: identifying component shapes, calculating each area using A = lw for rectangles and A = 1/2 bh for triangles, determining whether to add or subtract, and computing accurately. Vocabulary: compound figure, decompose, triangle, rectangle, area, base, height. Common misconceptions: using wrong dimensions for triangle base/height, not identifying all components, and confusing add/subtract. Practice should include figure analysis appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Measurement and data.skill: Area of compound figures with triangles, semicircles, and quarter circles
Maths - Measurement and data.prompt: This topic covers semicircle calculations for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.9(C)). Students calculate area, perimeter, radius, and diameter of semicircles. Key concepts include: area of a semicircle = (1/2)*pi*r^2, perimeter includes the curved part (pi*r) plus the diameter, and the relationship between semicircle and full circle measurements. Vocabulary: semicircle, area, perimeter, radius, diameter, pi, half circle, curved part, straight edge. Common misconceptions: forgetting to include the straight edge in perimeter, not halving the area formula, and confusing curved perimeter with full circumference. Practice should include computation and real-world problems appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Measurement and data.skill: Nets of prisms and pyramids
Maths - Measurement and data.prompt: This topic covers nets of prisms and pyramids for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.9(D)). Students identify, draw, and analyze two-dimensional nets that fold into three-dimensional prisms and pyramids. Key concepts include: understanding that a net shows all faces of a 3D figure laid flat, identifying which nets fold into specific solids, matching faces and edges, and using nets to calculate surface area. Vocabulary: net, face, edge, prism, pyramid, fold, three-dimensional, two-dimensional, surface area. Common misconceptions: not recognizing valid nets, confusing which edges connect when folded, and miscounting faces. Practice should include drawing and identifying nets appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Measurement and data.skill: Surface area of cubes and prisms
Maths - Measurement and data.prompt: This topic covers surface area of cubes and prisms for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.9(D)). Students calculate total surface area by finding and summing the areas of all faces. Key concepts include: identifying all faces (rectangles for prisms, squares for cubes), using SA = 2lw + 2lh + 2wh for rectangular prisms and SA = 6s^2 for cubes, and using nets to organize calculations. Vocabulary: surface area, face, cube, rectangular prism, net, square units, length, width, height. Common misconceptions: missing faces (especially opposite pairs), confusing surface area with volume, and using cubic instead of square units. Practice should include computation and word problems appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Measurement and data.skill: Surface area of pyramids
Maths - Measurement and data.prompt: This topic covers surface area of pyramids for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.9(D)). Students calculate total surface area by finding the base area plus the areas of all triangular lateral faces. Key concepts include: SA = base area + (1/2) x perimeter x slant height for regular pyramids, identifying the base shape, computing triangular face areas using A = 1/2 bh where h is the slant height, and summing all face areas. Vocabulary: surface area, pyramid, base, lateral face, slant height, triangle, square units. Common misconceptions: using perpendicular height instead of slant height, forgetting the base, and errors with non-square bases. Practice should include various pyramid types appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Measurement and data.skill: Lateral area of prisms and pyramids
Maths - Measurement and data.prompt: This topic covers lateral area of prisms and pyramids for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.9(D)). Students calculate the lateral surface area (area of the sides only, excluding bases). Key concepts include: lateral area of prisms = perimeter of base x height, lateral area of pyramids = (1/2) x perimeter of base x slant height, distinguishing lateral area from total surface area, and applying to real-world contexts (painting walls, wrapping). Vocabulary: lateral area, surface area, prism, pyramid, perimeter, height, slant height, base. Common misconceptions: including the base in lateral area, using wrong height measurement, and confusing lateral with total surface area. Practice should include computation and application problems appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Personal financial literacy.skill: Calculate sales tax for a given purchase
Maths - Personal financial literacy.prompt: This topic covers calculating sales tax for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.13(A)). Students compute sales tax and total cost including tax. Key concepts include: converting tax rate to decimal, multiplying by purchase price, and adding to find total. Vocabulary: sales tax, tax rate, purchase price, total cost, percent, decimal. Common misconceptions: adding tax rate directly and not converting percent. Practice should include shopping scenarios appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Personal financial literacy.skill: Calculate income tax for earned wages
Maths - Personal financial literacy.prompt: This topic covers calculating income tax on earned wages for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.13(A)). Students compute income tax amounts from wages and tax rates. Key concepts include: applying tax rate to wages, distinguishing gross from net pay, and understanding tax rates can vary. Vocabulary: income tax, wages, gross pay, net pay, tax rate, deduction, taxable income. Common misconceptions: confusing gross and net pay. Practice should include paycheck scenarios appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Personal financial literacy.skill: Identify components of a personal budget
Maths - Personal financial literacy.prompt: This topic covers identifying personal budget components for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.13(B)). Students learn to identify and categorize budget parts: income, fixed expenses (rent, insurance), variable expenses (food, entertainment), savings, and giving. Key concept: a balanced budget has expenses not exceeding income. Vocabulary: budget, income, expense, fixed expense, variable expense, savings, surplus, deficit. Common misconceptions: forgetting savings and not distinguishing fixed from variable. Practice should include creating and analyzing budgets appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Personal financial literacy.skill: Calculate percentage of each budget category
Maths - Personal financial literacy.prompt: This topic covers calculating budget category percentages for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.13(B)). Students find what percent each category is of the total. Key concepts include: dividing category amount by total, multiplying by 100, verifying percentages sum to ~100%, and analyzing reasonableness. Vocabulary: budget, category, percentage, proportion, total, allocate, pie chart. Common misconceptions: dividing by wrong total and rounding errors. Practice should include budget analysis appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Personal financial literacy.skill: Create financial records and net worth statements
Maths - Personal financial literacy.prompt: This topic covers creating financial records and net worth statements for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.13(C)). Students track assets and liabilities and calculate net worth = assets - liabilities. Key concepts include: categorizing items, assigning monetary values, computing totals, and interpreting positive/negative net worth. Vocabulary: financial record, net worth, asset, liability, debt, value, balance. Common misconceptions: confusing assets and liabilities. Practice should include personal financial statement creation appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Personal financial literacy.skill: Use budget estimators for household budgeting
Maths - Personal financial literacy.prompt: This topic covers using budget estimators for household budgeting for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.13(D)). Students use tools to estimate household expenses and create realistic budgets. Key concepts include: identifying typical expenses (housing, utilities, food, transportation), estimating costs, balancing income against expenses, and adjusting categories. Vocabulary: budget estimator, household budget, expenses, income, estimate, financial planning. Common misconceptions: underestimating variable expenses and not accounting for irregular costs. Practice should include realistic budgeting scenarios appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

This topic covers calculating simple interest for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.13(E)). Students use I = Prt where P is principal, r is annual rate (as decimal), and t is time in years. Key concepts include: converting rate to decimal, computing interest, finding total amount, and comparing scenarios. Vocabulary: simple interest, principal, rate, time, interest earned, total amount, annual, percent. Common misconceptions: not converting rate, confusing simple and compound interest, and using months without converting to years. Practice should include savings and loan scenarios appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

This topic covers calculating compound interest for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.13(E)). Students learn compound interest is calculated on principal and previously earned interest. Formula: A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt). Key concepts include: compound interest grows faster than simple, computing for given periods, and comparing outcomes. Vocabulary: compound interest, principal, rate, compounding period, annually, monthly, exponential growth. Common misconceptions: confusing with simple interest and errors with exponents. Practice should include savings and investment scenarios appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

This topic covers analyzing monetary incentives (sales, rebates, coupons) for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.13(F)). Students evaluate promotions to make informed purchasing decisions. Key concepts include: calculating savings from each type, comparing dollar-off vs. percent-off, understanding rebate processes, and determining best deals. Vocabulary: incentive, sale, rebate, coupon, discount, savings, percent off, dollar off, promotion. Common misconceptions: assuming highest percent off is always best. Practice should include consumer comparison scenarios appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

This topic covers solving percent equations for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.4(D)). Students solve for the part, whole, or percent using the equation part = percent x whole. Key concepts include: converting between percents, decimals, and fractions, solving for each variable, and applying to financial contexts. Vocabulary: percent, equation, part, whole, solve, convert, decimal, fraction. Common misconceptions: confusing part and whole and not converting percent to decimal. Practice should include financial math contexts appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

This topic covers calculating unit prices for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.4(B)). Students find the cost per single unit by dividing total price by quantity. Key concepts include: setting up the division, comparing unit prices for better value, and applying to shopping decisions. Vocabulary: unit price, cost, total price, quantity, compare, value, per unit, best deal. Common misconceptions: dividing in the wrong order and not comparing when package sizes differ. Practice should include grocery and consumer scenarios appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Personal financial literacy.skill: Percent of a number: tax, discount, and more
Maths - Personal financial literacy.prompt: This topic covers calculating tax, discount, and related percent applications for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.13(A)). Students find a percent of a number in contexts like sales tax, discounts, tips, markups, and commissions. Key concepts include: converting percent to decimal, multiplying by the base amount, adding tax or subtracting discount, and computing final prices. Vocabulary: percent, tax, discount, tip, markup, commission, original price, sale price, total cost. Common misconceptions: adding the percent itself instead of the calculated amount and confusing tax (added) with discount (subtracted). Practice should include consumer math scenarios appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

This topic covers comparing coupons to find the better deal for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.13(F)). Students compare percent-off coupons with dollar-off coupons. Key concepts include: calculating dollar value of a percent discount, comparing savings, understanding that the better coupon depends on purchase price, and analyzing each type. Vocabulary: coupon, discount, percent off, dollar off, savings, compare, better deal. Common misconceptions: assuming higher percent is always better without calculating. Practice should include shopping comparison scenarios appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Personal financial literacy.skill: Find the percent: tax, discount, and more
Maths - Personal financial literacy.prompt: This topic covers finding the percent in tax, discount, and related scenarios for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.13(A)). Students determine the percent when given the part and whole in consumer contexts. Key concepts include: percent = (part/whole) x 100, identifying part and whole, and interpreting in context. Vocabulary: percent, part, whole, tax rate, discount rate, tip percent, original price, amount saved. Common misconceptions: dividing in wrong order, confusing part and whole, and not multiplying by 100. Practice should include consumer math appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Personal financial literacy.skill: Sale prices: find the original price
Maths - Personal financial literacy.prompt: This topic covers finding the original price from a sale price for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.13(F)). Students work backward from a discounted price and discount percent. Key concept: sale price = original x (1 - d/100), so original = sale price / (1 - d/100). Vocabulary: original price, sale price, discount, percent off, solve, work backward. Common misconceptions: simply adding the discount percent to the sale price and dividing by percent instead of (1 - d/100). Practice should include realistic shopping scenarios appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

This topic covers multi-step problems with percents for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.4(D)). Students solve complex problems requiring multiple percent calculations, such as discount then tax, or successive percent changes. Key concepts include: performing calculations in correct order, understanding successive changes are not additive, and tracking intermediate values. Vocabulary: percent, multi-step, discount, tax, tip, markup, successive change, compound. Common misconceptions: adding successive percents, wrong order of operations, and using wrong base. Practice should include layered consumer math appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Probability and statistics.skill: Represent sample spaces for simple events using lists and tree diagrams
Maths - Probability and statistics.prompt: This topic covers representing sample spaces for simple events for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.6(A)). Students list all possible outcomes using lists and tree diagrams. Key concepts include: defining event/outcome, creating organized lists, using tree diagrams, and connecting to probability. Vocabulary: sample space, event, outcome, list, tree diagram, probability, equally likely. Common misconceptions: listing outcomes incompletely and confusing events with outcomes. Practice should include dice, coins, spinners, and card scenarios appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Probability and statistics.skill: Represent sample spaces for compound events using lists and tree diagrams
Maths - Probability and statistics.prompt: This topic covers representing sample spaces for compound events for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.6(A)). Students list all outcomes when two or more events occur together. Key concepts include: using tree diagrams, tables, and lists, applying the counting principle, and determining total outcomes. Vocabulary: compound event, sample space, tree diagram, counting principle, outcome, combination. Common misconceptions: not listing all combinations and double-counting. Practice should include multi-step scenarios appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Probability and statistics.skill: Find the number of outcomes for compound events
Maths - Probability and statistics.prompt: This topic covers finding the number of outcomes for compound events for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.6(A)). Students use the Fundamental Counting Principle: multiply the outcomes for each event. Key concepts include: identifying options for each event, multiplying across events, and applying to menus, outfits, passwords. Vocabulary: compound event, Fundamental Counting Principle, outcome, multiply, total, combination. Common misconceptions: adding instead of multiplying. Practice should include menu, clothing, and code scenarios appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Probability and statistics.skill: Select and use different simulations to represent events
Maths - Probability and statistics.prompt: This topic covers selecting and using simulations for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.6(B)). Students choose appropriate tools (coins, dice, spinners, random generators) to model probability situations. Key concepts include: matching outcomes in the simulation to the real event, running multiple trials, recording results, and estimating probabilities. Vocabulary: simulation, model, trial, random, equally likely, experimental probability. Common misconceptions: choosing simulations with wrong number of outcomes and not running enough trials. Practice should include designing simulations appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

This topic covers making predictions using experimental probability for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.6(C)). Students use experiment results to estimate probabilities and predict future outcomes. Key concepts include: calculating experimental probability = favorable outcomes / total trials, using it to predict future results, and understanding more trials improve reliability. Vocabulary: experimental probability, trial, outcome, predict, frequency, relative frequency. Common misconceptions: expecting exact match with theoretical probability and using too few trials. Practice should include data-based predictions appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

This topic covers determining solutions using experimental probability for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.6(C)). Students use experimental data to solve problems and make decisions. Key concepts include: conducting experiments, computing probabilities, using proportional reasoning to scale predictions, and evaluating reliability. Vocabulary: experimental probability, data, trial, outcome, solution, frequency, proportion. Common misconceptions: treating small samples as conclusive and confusing experimental with theoretical probability. Practice should include experiment-based problem solving appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

This topic covers making predictions using theoretical probability for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.6(D)). Students use theoretical probability (equally likely outcomes) to predict results. Key concepts include: P = favorable/total, multiplying by trial count for expected frequency, and understanding variability. Vocabulary: theoretical probability, equally likely, favorable outcome, predict, expected, frequency. Common misconceptions: confusing with experimental probability and thinking predictions are guarantees. Practice should include dice, coin, spinner problems appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Probability and statistics.skill: Find probabilities of simple events and their complements
Maths - Probability and statistics.prompt: This topic covers probabilities of simple events and complements for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.6(E)). Key relationship: P(event) + P(complement) = 1. Students find probabilities of events and their complements. Key concepts include: identifying the complement, using the complement rule efficiently, and applying in context. Vocabulary: probability, event, complement, P(A), P(not A), certain, impossible. Common misconceptions: not recognizing the complement relationship. Practice should include varied probability scenarios appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

This topic covers estimating population size using proportions for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.6(F)). Students use sample data and proportional reasoning to estimate total populations, similar to capture-recapture methods. Key concepts include: setting up proportions, cross-multiplying, understanding sampling assumptions. Vocabulary: population, sample, proportion, estimate, capture-recapture, representative. Common misconceptions: not recognizing when proportions apply and cross-multiplication errors. Practice should include wildlife estimation and survey problems appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Probability and statistics.skill: Solve problems using data in bar graphs, dot plots, and circle graphs
Maths - Probability and statistics.prompt: This topic covers solving problems using bar graphs, dot plots, and circle graphs for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.6(G)). Students read, interpret, and use data from various displays. Key concepts include: reading values from bar graphs, identifying frequencies in dot plots, interpreting proportions in circle graphs, and computing statistics. Vocabulary: bar graph, dot plot, circle graph, frequency, category, proportion. Common misconceptions: misreading scales and confusing frequency with value. Practice should include multi-graph interpretation appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

This topic covers interpreting line plots with fractions for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.6(G)). Students read and analyze line plots where data values are fractions. Key concepts include: reading fractional values, counting data points, computing totals, and finding mean/median of fractional data. Vocabulary: line plot, dot plot, fraction, frequency, number line, mean, median. Common misconceptions: miscounting points and errors adding fractions. Practice should include measurement data appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

This topic covers interpreting bar graphs for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.6(G)). Students read and analyze bar graphs to answer questions and draw conclusions. Key concepts include: reading scale and labels, comparing bar heights, computing totals and averages, and making inferences. Vocabulary: bar graph, axis, scale, category, frequency, compare, interpret. Common misconceptions: misreading scale intervals and making unsupported claims. Practice should include varied bar graph contexts appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

This topic covers interpreting circle graphs (pie charts) for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.6(G)). Students determine proportions, calculate actual values, and make comparisons from circle graphs. Key concepts include: whole circle = 100%, reading sectors, computing actual values from percents, and comparing sectors. Vocabulary: circle graph, pie chart, sector, percent, proportion, whole, part. Common misconceptions: not understanding sectors as parts of whole and errors converting percents to values. Practice should include survey and budget data appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

This topic covers estimating population size using proportions for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.6(F)). Students use sample data and proportional reasoning to estimate total populations, similar to capture-recapture methods. Key concepts include: setting up proportions, cross-multiplying, understanding sampling assumptions. Vocabulary: population, sample, proportion, estimate, capture-recapture, representative. Common misconceptions: not recognizing when proportions apply and cross-multiplication errors. Practice should include wildlife estimation and survey problems appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Probability and statistics.skill: Make predictions using experimental and theoretical probability
Maths - Probability and statistics.prompt: This topic covers making predictions using both experimental and theoretical probability for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.6(I)). Students compare both approaches and use them for informed predictions. Key concepts include: computing theoretical probability from equally likely outcomes, computing experimental probability from data, understanding convergence with more trials (Law of Large Numbers), and choosing appropriate methods. Vocabulary: experimental probability, theoretical probability, prediction, Law of Large Numbers, converge. Common misconceptions: expecting exact match and not recognizing when to use each type. Practice should include comparative exercises appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Probability and statistics.skill: Determine experimental and theoretical probabilities for events
Maths - Probability and statistics.prompt: This topic covers determining both experimental and theoretical probabilities for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.6(I)). Students calculate both and compare results. Key concepts include: theoretical = favorable/total (equally likely), experimental = favorable occurrences/trials, comparing and discussing discrepancies. Vocabulary: experimental probability, theoretical probability, favorable outcome, trial, frequency. Common misconceptions: thinking they must always match and using theoretical when outcomes are not equally likely. Practice should include experiments with known theoretical values appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

This topic covers identifying independent and dependent events for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.6(I)). Students distinguish between events where one outcome affects the other (dependent) and events where outcomes are unrelated (independent). Key concepts include: independent events (coin flips), dependent events (drawing without replacement), and how the sample space changes. Vocabulary: independent events, dependent events, probability, with/without replacement, sample space. Common misconceptions: thinking all sequential events are dependent and confusing replacement concepts. Practice should include card, marble, and spinner scenarios appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Maths - Probability and statistics.skill: Find probabilities of independent and dependent events
Maths - Probability and statistics.prompt: This topic covers finding probabilities of independent and dependent events for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.6(I)). For independent: P(A and B) = P(A) x P(B). For dependent: P(A and B) = P(A) x P(B|A). Key concepts include: applying the multiplication rule, adjusting for dependent events, and multi-step problems. Vocabulary: independent, dependent, compound probability, multiplication rule, conditional probability, replacement. Common misconceptions: using independent formula for dependent events and not adjusting denominators. Practice should include drawing and sequential event problems appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Laboratory safety: Demonstrate safe practices during laboratory and field investigations as outlined in TEA-approved safety standards
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Use appropriate safety equipment and practices during laboratory, classroom, and field investigations
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers laboratory safety practices for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.1(C)). Students demonstrate safe practices during laboratory and field investigations following TEA-approved safety standards. Key concepts include: wearing PPE (goggles, aprons, gloves), proper chemical and specimen handling, knowing safety equipment locations (fire extinguisher, eyewash station, fire blanket), following instructions, and reporting accidents. Vocabulary: safety goggles, apron, fire extinguisher, eyewash station, MSDS, hazard, precaution, PPE. Common misconceptions: thinking safety rules are optional and underestimating chemical hazards. Practice should include safety scenario analysis appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Conservation of resources: Practice appropriate use and conservation of resources, including disposal, reuse, or recycling of materials
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Use appropriate safety equipment and practices during investigations (includes conservation of resources)
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers conservation of resources in science investigations for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.1(C)). Students practice appropriate use and conservation of materials including proper disposal, reuse, and recycling. Key concepts include: minimizing waste, properly disposing chemicals and biological materials, reusing when safe, recycling, and environmental impact awareness. Vocabulary: conservation, disposal, reuse, recycle, waste, resource, environmental impact, sustainable, hazardous waste. Common misconceptions: thinking all lab waste goes in regular trash. Practice should include lab cleanup scenarios appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Scientific investigations: Plan and implement comparative and descriptive investigations by making observations, asking well-defined questions, and using appropriate equipment and technology
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Use scientific practices to plan and conduct investigations
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers planning comparative and descriptive investigations for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.1(B)). Students design investigations that compare groups or describe phenomena. Key concepts include: comparative investigations (comparing groups) vs. descriptive (observing without manipulation), systematic observations, clear research questions, and appropriate equipment. Vocabulary: comparative investigation, descriptive investigation, observation, research question, equipment, data, systematic, variable. Common misconceptions: confusing descriptive with experimental and not controlling variables. Practice should include designing both types appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Scientific investigations: The process of scientific inquiry
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Ask questions and define problems based on observations or information
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers the process of scientific inquiry for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.1(A)). Students understand the steps and nature of scientific investigation: observing, asking testable questions, forming hypotheses, designing experiments, collecting/analyzing data, drawing conclusions, and communicating results. Vocabulary: observation, hypothesis, experiment, data, conclusion, variable, control, testable question, evidence. Common misconceptions: thinking there is one rigid scientific method and that hypotheses must be correct. Practice should include identifying inquiry steps appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Scientific investigations: Identify laboratory tools
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Use appropriate tools for observation and measurement
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers identifying laboratory tools for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.1(D)). Students recognize common lab instruments and their uses: graduated cylinder (liquid volume), beaker (holding liquids), triple beam balance (mass), thermometer (temperature), microscope (magnification), spring scale (force), hot plate, test tubes, Erlenmeyer flask, forceps. Vocabulary: graduated cylinder, beaker, triple beam balance, thermometer, microscope, spring scale, Erlenmeyer flask, pipette, forceps. Common misconceptions: confusing beakers with graduated cylinders for measurement and misreading meniscus. Practice should include identification exercises appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Experimental design: Design and implement experimental investigations by making observations, asking well-defined questions, formulating testable hypotheses, and using appropriate equipment and technology
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Use scientific practices to plan and conduct investigations
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers designing and implementing experimental investigations for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.1(B)). Students create experiments with testable hypotheses, controlled variables, and appropriate methodology. Key concepts include: formulating testable hypotheses, identifying independent/dependent/controlled variables, designing procedures, selecting equipment, collecting data, and drawing evidence-based conclusions. Vocabulary: experiment, hypothesis, independent variable, dependent variable, controlled variable, procedure, data, conclusion. Common misconceptions: not controlling variables, confusing correlation with causation, and writing untestable hypotheses. Practice should include experimental design scenarios appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Experimental design: Identify control and experimental groups
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Use scientific practices to plan investigations (identifying controls and variables)
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers identifying control and experimental groups for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.1(B)). Students distinguish between the group that receives the treatment (experimental) and the group that serves as the baseline (control). Key concepts include: the control group provides a comparison standard, the experimental group receives the independent variable treatment, keeping all other conditions the same, and understanding why controls are necessary. Vocabulary: control group, experimental group, independent variable, treatment, baseline, comparison, constant. Common misconceptions: thinking the control group is unnecessary and confusing which group receives the treatment. Practice should include identifying groups in scenarios appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Experimental design: Identify independent and dependent variables
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Use scientific practices to plan investigations (identifying variables)
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers identifying independent and dependent variables in scientific investigations for Grade 7 students (TEKS 7.1(B)). Students distinguish between the variable changed by the experimenter (independent) and the variable measured as a response (dependent). Key concepts include: the independent variable is what is changed on purpose, the dependent variable is what is measured/observed, controlled variables are kept constant, and the dependent variable depends on the independent variable. Vocabulary: independent variable, dependent variable, controlled variable, constant, experiment, respond, manipulate. Common misconceptions: reversing independent and dependent and forgetting controlled variables. Practice should include identifying variables in experiments appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Experimental design: Identify the experimental question
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Ask questions and define problems based on observations
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers identifying the experimental question for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.1(A)). Students formulate clear, testable questions that can be investigated through experiments. Key concepts include: a good experimental question identifies what is being tested, specifies the variables, can be answered through data collection, and leads to a clear hypothesis. Vocabulary: experimental question, testable, hypothesis, variable, investigation, predict, observe. Common misconceptions: writing questions that are too broad or untestable and confusing questions with hypotheses. Practice should include evaluating and writing questions appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Experimental design: Identify questions that can be investigated with a set of materials
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Ask questions and define problems based on observations
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers identifying questions that can be investigated with given materials for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.1(A)). Students evaluate which questions are testable given a specific set of materials and equipment. Key concepts include: matching questions to available resources, understanding limitations of materials, designing feasible investigations, and distinguishing testable from untestable questions. Vocabulary: testable, investigation, materials, equipment, feasible, limitation, observation, measurement. Common misconceptions: thinking any question can be tested with any materials and not considering practical constraints. Practice should include evaluating questions against material lists appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Experimental design: Understand an experimental protocol about plant growth
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Use scientific practices to plan investigations
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers understanding experimental protocols about plant growth for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.1(B)). Students analyze and interpret a detailed experimental procedure studying factors affecting plant growth. Key concepts include: identifying variables (light, water, soil, fertilizer), understanding the control setup, interpreting data tables and graphs of growth measurements, and drawing conclusions about what factors affect plant growth. Vocabulary: protocol, plant growth, variable, control, measurement, data, conclusion, photosynthesis, germination. Common misconceptions: thinking plants only need sunlight (forgetting water, nutrients) and not controlling variables properly. Practice should include protocol analysis appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Experimental design: Understand an experimental protocol about diffusion
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Use scientific practices to plan investigations
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers understanding experimental protocols about diffusion for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.1(B)). Students analyze experiments studying the movement of particles from high to low concentration. Key concepts include: diffusion as passive movement of molecules, concentration gradients, factors affecting diffusion rate (temperature, concentration difference, particle size), experimental setup with indicators, and data interpretation. Vocabulary: diffusion, concentration, gradient, particles, molecules, membrane, indicator, rate, passive transport. Common misconceptions: thinking diffusion requires energy and that it stops when equilibrium is reached (it continues but net movement is zero). Practice should include protocol analysis appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Data collection: Collect and record data using the International System of Units (SI) and qualitative means such as labeled drawings, writing, and graphic organizers
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Collect quantitative and qualitative data using SI units
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers collecting and recording data using SI units for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.1(E)). Students use the International System of Units (metric system) and qualitative methods to collect and organize data. Key concepts include: using SI units (meters, kilograms, liters, degrees Celsius), recording data in organized tables, using labeled drawings and graphic organizers, distinguishing quantitative from qualitative data, and appropriate significant figures. Vocabulary: SI units, metric system, meter, kilogram, liter, Celsius, qualitative, quantitative, data table. Common misconceptions: confusing SI with customary units and not recording units with measurements. Practice should include data collection activities appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Data collection: Choose metric units of distance, mass, and volume
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Collect quantitative and qualitative data using SI units
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers choosing appropriate metric units for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.1(E)). Students select the correct metric unit for measuring distance, mass, and volume in various situations. Key units include: distance (millimeters, centimeters, meters, kilometers), mass (milligrams, grams, kilograms), volume (milliliters, liters), and understanding the relative sizes of each. Vocabulary: metric, meter, centimeter, millimeter, kilometer, gram, kilogram, milligram, liter, milliliter, unit. Common misconceptions: using inappropriate units (e.g., kilometers for pencil length) and confusing mass with weight. Practice should include matching units to measurements appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Data analysis: Construct tables and graphs, using repeated trials and means, to organize data and identify patterns
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Construct tables, graphs, maps, and charts using repeated trials
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers constructing tables and graphs for data analysis for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.1(F)). Students organize data into tables and graphs to identify patterns. Key concepts include: creating data tables with proper labels and units, choosing appropriate graph types (bar, line, circle), using repeated trials and calculating means, labeling axes and titles, and identifying trends and patterns in graphed data. Vocabulary: data table, graph, bar graph, line graph, axis, label, title, mean, trend, pattern, trial. Common misconceptions: not labeling axes, choosing inappropriate graph types, and not using consistent scales. Practice should include data organization exercises appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Data analysis: Use data from tests to compare engineering-design solutions
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Evaluate experimental and engineering designs
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers using data to compare engineering design solutions for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.2(D)). Students analyze test data to evaluate and compare different engineering designs. Key concepts include: defining criteria and constraints, collecting performance data, comparing designs using data tables and graphs, identifying trade-offs, and selecting the best solution based on evidence. Vocabulary: engineering design, criteria, constraints, data, compare, evaluate, trade-off, solution, performance, evidence. Common misconceptions: choosing designs based on preference rather than data and not considering all criteria. Practice should include design comparison scenarios appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Data analysis: Analyze data to formulate reasonable explanations, communicate valid conclusions supported by the data, and predict trends
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Analyze data by identifying statistical features and patterns
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers analyzing data to formulate explanations and predict trends for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.2(B)). Students use data analysis to develop evidence-based explanations and make predictions. Key concepts include: identifying patterns and trends in data, formulating explanations supported by evidence, distinguishing conclusions from inferences, communicating findings clearly, and predicting future trends. Vocabulary: analyze, data, explanation, conclusion, trend, pattern, evidence, predict, inference. Common misconceptions: confusing correlation with causation, making claims not supported by data, and over-generalizing from limited data. Practice should include data interpretation scenarios appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Critical thinking: Evaluate claims about natural resource use: groundwater
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Make informed decisions by evaluating evidence from multiple sources
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers evaluating claims about natural resource use for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.4(B)). Students critically evaluate evidence-based claims about how humans use natural resources, particularly groundwater. Key concepts include: understanding groundwater systems (aquifers, recharge, depletion), evaluating evidence quality, identifying bias in claims, and making informed decisions about resource management. Vocabulary: groundwater, aquifer, recharge, depletion, natural resource, claim, evidence, bias, sustainability. Common misconceptions: thinking groundwater is unlimited, not understanding recharge rates, and accepting claims without evidence. Practice should include evaluating resource claims appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Critical thinking: Factors affecting climate: altitude
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Make informed decisions by evaluating evidence from multiple sources
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers how altitude affects climate for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.4(B)). Students evaluate evidence about how altitude influences temperature and climate patterns. Key concepts include: temperature decreases with altitude (approximately 6.5 degrees C per 1000m), the effect on precipitation (orographic effect), treeline and snow line, and why mountains have different climate zones at different elevations. Vocabulary: altitude, elevation, temperature, climate, lapse rate, orographic effect, treeline, precipitation. Common misconceptions: confusing altitude with latitude and thinking altitude only affects temperature. Practice should include evidence evaluation about altitude effects appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Critical thinking: Analyze data to compare properties of planets
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Use mathematical calculations to assess quantitative relationships
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers analyzing data to compare planetary properties for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.2(C)). Students use mathematical calculations to compare properties of planets in our solar system. Key concepts include: comparing diameter, mass, distance from the Sun, orbital period, number of moons, and surface conditions, using ratios and proportional reasoning, and organizing data for comparison. Vocabulary: planet, diameter, mass, orbit, orbital period, terrestrial, gas giant, solar system, ratio. Common misconceptions: thinking planet size correlates with distance from the Sun and confusing orbital period with rotation period. Practice should include data comparison appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Models: Use models to represent aspects of the natural world such as human body systems and plant and animal cells
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Develop and use models to represent phenomena and systems
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers using models to represent natural phenomena for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.1(G)). Students develop and use physical, mathematical, and conceptual models to represent and understand complex systems. Key concepts include: understanding that models are simplified representations, identifying what a model shows and its limitations, using models to make predictions, and revising models based on new evidence. Vocabulary: model, representation, prediction, limitation, system, phenomena, simplified, accurate. Common misconceptions: thinking models are exact replicas and not recognizing their limitations. Practice should include model analysis and creation appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Models: Plant cell diagrams - label parts
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Develop and use models to represent phenomena and systems
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers labeling plant cell diagrams for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.1(G)). Students identify and label the key structures of a plant cell. Key structures include: cell wall, cell membrane, nucleus, chloroplast, large central vacuole, mitochondria, endoplasmic reticulum (smooth and rough), Golgi body, ribosomes, and cytoplasm. Key concepts include: understanding each organelle's function, recognizing structures unique to plant cells (cell wall, chloroplast, large vacuole), and comparing with animal cells. Vocabulary: cell wall, cell membrane, nucleus, chloroplast, vacuole, mitochondria, endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi body, ribosome, cytoplasm. Common misconceptions: confusing cell wall with cell membrane and thinking animal cells have chloroplasts. Practice should include labeling and function matching appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Models: Animal cell diagrams - label parts
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Develop and use models to represent phenomena and systems
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers labeling animal cell diagrams for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.1(G)). Students identify and label the key structures of an animal cell. Key structures include: cell membrane, nucleus, mitochondria, endoplasmic reticulum (smooth and rough), Golgi body, ribosomes, lysosomes, cytoplasm, and centrioles. Key concepts include: understanding each organelle's function, recognizing structures unique to animal cells (centrioles, lysosomes, no cell wall), and comparing with plant cells. Vocabulary: cell membrane, nucleus, mitochondria, lysosome, centriole, endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi body, ribosome, cytoplasm. Common misconceptions: thinking animal cells have cell walls and confusing organelle functions. Practice should include labeling activities appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Models: Organization in the human body
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Develop and use models to represent phenomena and systems
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers modeling organization in the human body for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.1(G)). Students use models to represent the hierarchical organization from cells to the organism level. Key concepts include: cells as the basic unit of life, cells forming tissues, tissues forming organs, organs forming organ systems, organ systems working together in the organism, and examples at each level. Vocabulary: cell, tissue, organ, organ system, organism, hierarchy, organization, model. Common misconceptions: thinking all cells are the same and not understanding how levels connect. Practice should include creating and interpreting models appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Advantages and limitations of models: Analyze models of the Earth-Sun-Moon system
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Identify advantages and limitations of models
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers analyzing models of the Earth-Sun-Moon system for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.2(A)). Students evaluate models that represent the relationships and movements within this system. Key concepts include: Earth's rotation (day/night), Earth's revolution around the Sun (seasons), Moon's revolution around Earth (lunar phases), eclipses, and the advantages and limitations of various models. Vocabulary: rotation, revolution, orbit, axis, tilt, eclipse, lunar phases, model, scale, limitation. Common misconceptions: thinking seasons are caused by distance from the Sun, confusing rotation with revolution, and not recognizing model limitations. Practice should include model evaluation appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Impact of research: Explore the engineering-design process: going to the Moon!
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Relate impact of past and current research on scientific thought and society
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers the engineering design process through the context of lunar exploration for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.4(A)). Students explore how engineering design was applied to space missions. Key concepts include: the engineering design process (identify problem, research, design, build, test, redesign), challenges of space travel (no atmosphere, extreme temperatures, radiation, microgravity), historical Moon missions, and how scientific research impacts society. Vocabulary: engineering design, prototype, criteria, constraint, iteration, spacecraft, lunar, mission, technology. Common misconceptions: thinking engineering design is linear and that first designs are always final. Practice should include design challenge activities appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Laboratory tools: Identify laboratory tools
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Use appropriate tools for observation and measurement
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers identifying laboratory tools for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.1(D)). Students recognize common lab instruments and their uses: graduated cylinder (liquid volume), beaker (holding liquids), triple beam balance (mass), thermometer (temperature), microscope (magnification), spring scale (force), hot plate, test tubes, Erlenmeyer flask, forceps. Vocabulary: graduated cylinder, beaker, triple beam balance, thermometer, microscope, spring scale, Erlenmeyer flask, pipette, forceps. Common misconceptions: confusing beakers with graduated cylinders for measurement and misreading meniscus. Practice should include identification exercises appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Scientific investigation and r.skill: Safety equipment: Laboratory safety equipment
Science - Scientific investigation and r.teks_description: Use appropriate safety equipment and practices during investigations
Science - Scientific investigation and r.prompt: This topic covers laboratory safety equipment for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.1(C)). Students identify the purpose and proper use of common safety equipment in the laboratory. Key equipment includes: safety goggles (eye protection), lab apron (body protection), fire extinguisher, fire blanket, eyewash station, safety shower, fume hood, first aid kit, and broken glass container. Key concepts include: knowing when each piece of equipment is needed, proper usage procedures, and equipment locations. Vocabulary: safety goggles, apron, fire extinguisher, fire blanket, eyewash station, fume hood, first aid kit. Common misconceptions: thinking safety equipment is only for emergencies. Practice should include safety equipment identification and scenarios appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Matter and energy.skill: Photosynthesis: Recognize that radiant energy from the Sun is transformed into chemical energy through photosynthesis
Science - Matter and energy.teks_description: Compare and contrast elements and compounds; understand energy transformations in matter
Science - Matter and energy.prompt: This topic covers the transformation of solar energy into chemical energy through photosynthesis for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.6(A)). Students understand how plants convert radiant (light) energy from the Sun into chemical energy stored in glucose. Key concepts include: the photosynthesis equation (6CO2 + 6H2O + light energy -> C6H12O6 + 6O2), chloroplasts as the site of photosynthesis, chlorophyll absorbing light energy, and the role of photosynthesis in ecosystems. Vocabulary: photosynthesis, radiant energy, chemical energy, chloroplast, chlorophyll, glucose, carbon dioxide, oxygen, transformation. Common misconceptions: thinking plants get food from the soil and that photosynthesis only produces oxygen. Practice should include energy transformation analysis appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Matter and energy.skill: Photosynthesis: How do plants use and change energy?
Science - Matter and energy.teks_description: Analyze how energy flows and matter cycles through systems
Science - Matter and energy.prompt: This topic covers how plants use and change energy for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.5(E)). Students explore the energy transformations that occur in plants. Key concepts include: plants capture light energy and convert it to chemical energy through photosynthesis, plants use chemical energy through cellular respiration, energy flows from sunlight to producers to consumers, and the relationship between photosynthesis and respiration. Vocabulary: photosynthesis, cellular respiration, chemical energy, light energy, thermal energy, glucose, ATP, producer. Common misconceptions: thinking plants only photosynthesize (they also respire), and that photosynthesis and respiration are opposites rather than complementary. Practice should include energy flow analysis appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Matter and energy.teks_description: Diagram energy flow within trophic levels in energy pyramids
Science - Matter and energy.prompt: This topic covers identifying photosynthetic organisms for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.12(A)). Students learn to identify which organisms perform photosynthesis and their role in energy pyramids. Key concepts include: producers (plants, algae, some bacteria) perform photosynthesis, they form the base of food chains and energy pyramids, not all green organisms photosynthesize, and photosynthetic organisms convert sunlight to chemical energy. Vocabulary: photosynthetic, producer, autotroph, chlorophyll, energy pyramid, trophic level, food chain, algae, cyanobacteria. Common misconceptions: thinking only land plants photosynthesize and confusing producers with consumers. Practice should include organism classification appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Matter and energy.skill: Energy flow: Diagram the flow of energy through living systems, including food chains, food webs, and energy pyramids
Science - Matter and energy.teks_description: Diagram energy flow within trophic levels in energy pyramids
Science - Matter and energy.prompt: This topic covers diagramming energy flow through living systems for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.12(A)). Students create and interpret food chains, food webs, and energy pyramids. Key concepts include: energy flows from sun to producers to consumers, only about 10% of energy transfers between trophic levels, energy pyramids show decreasing energy at higher levels, and decomposers recycle matter. Vocabulary: energy flow, food chain, food web, energy pyramid, trophic level, producer, consumer, decomposer, herbivore, carnivore, omnivore. Common misconceptions: thinking energy is recycled (it flows one way) and confusing energy flow with matter cycling. Practice should include diagram creation and interpretation appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Matter and energy.skill: Energy flow: How does matter move in food chains?
Science - Matter and energy.teks_description: Describe how ecosystems sustained by continuous energy flow and matter recycling
Science - Matter and energy.prompt: This topic covers how matter moves through food chains for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.12(B)). Students trace the movement of matter (not just energy) through ecosystems. Key concepts include: matter is recycled through ecosystems while energy flows through, organisms consume matter and transform it, decomposers break down dead matter returning nutrients to the environment, and the conservation of matter in ecosystems. Vocabulary: matter, food chain, nutrient cycle, decomposer, consumer, producer, recycle, conservation of matter, ecosystem. Common misconceptions: confusing matter cycling with energy flow and thinking matter is created or destroyed. Practice should include matter tracking activities appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Matter and energy.skill: Energy flow: Interpret food webs I
Science - Matter and energy.teks_description: Diagram energy flow within trophic levels in energy pyramids
Science - Matter and energy.prompt: This topic covers interpreting food webs for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.12(A)). Students analyze complex food web diagrams to understand energy relationships in ecosystems. Key concepts include: arrows show the direction of energy flow, organisms can occupy multiple trophic levels, removing one organism affects the entire web, and food webs are more realistic than food chains. Vocabulary: food web, food chain, producer, consumer, predator, prey, trophic level, interdependence, ecosystem. Common misconceptions: thinking arrows point toward the eater (they show energy direction), and not understanding cascading effects of population changes. Practice should include food web analysis appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Matter and energy.skill: Energy flow: Interpret food webs II
Science - Matter and energy.teks_description: Diagram energy flow within trophic levels in energy pyramids
Science - Matter and energy.prompt: This topic covers interpreting food webs for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.12(A)). Students analyze complex food web diagrams to understand energy relationships in ecosystems. Key concepts include: arrows show the direction of energy flow, organisms can occupy multiple trophic levels, removing one organism affects the entire web, and food webs are more realistic than food chains. Vocabulary: food web, food chain, producer, consumer, predator, prey, trophic level, interdependence, ecosystem. Common misconceptions: thinking arrows point toward the eater (they show energy direction), and not understanding cascading effects of population changes. Practice should include food web analysis appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Matter and energy.teks_description: Describe how ecosystems sustained by continuous energy flow and matter recycling
Science - Matter and energy.prompt: This topic covers the carbon cycle for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.12(B)). Students trace how carbon moves through ecosystems and between living and non-living components. Key concepts include: carbon dioxide absorbed by plants during photosynthesis, carbon stored in organisms and fossil fuels, carbon released through respiration, decomposition, and combustion, and the role of oceans as carbon sinks. Vocabulary: carbon cycle, photosynthesis, respiration, decomposition, combustion, fossil fuels, carbon dioxide, carbon sink, atmosphere. Common misconceptions: thinking the carbon cycle only involves living things and not understanding human impact on the cycle. Practice should include carbon cycle diagramming appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Matter and energy.skill: Physical and chemical changes: Distinguish between physical and chemical changes in matter
Science - Matter and energy.teks_description: Distinguish between physical and chemical changes
Science - Matter and energy.prompt: This topic covers distinguishing between physical and chemical changes in matter for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.6(C)). Students identify and compare changes that alter appearance only (physical) versus changes that create new substances (chemical). Key concepts include: physical changes (melting, dissolving, cutting) do not create new substances, chemical changes (burning, rusting, cooking) produce new substances with different properties, evidence of chemical change (color change, gas production, temperature change, precipitate), and reversibility. Vocabulary: physical change, chemical change, substance, property, reversible, irreversible, precipitate, reaction. Common misconceptions: thinking all color changes are chemical and confusing dissolving (physical) with a chemical reaction. Practice should include classification activities appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Matter and energy.skill: Physical and chemical changes: Compare physical and chemical changes
Science - Matter and energy.teks_description: Distinguish between physical and chemical changes
Science - Matter and energy.prompt: This topic covers distinguishing between physical and chemical changes in matter for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.6(C)). Students identify and compare changes that alter appearance only (physical) versus changes that create new substances (chemical). Key concepts include: physical changes (melting, dissolving, cutting) do not create new substances, chemical changes (burning, rusting, cooking) produce new substances with different properties, evidence of chemical change (color change, gas production, temperature change, precipitate), and reversibility. Vocabulary: physical change, chemical change, substance, property, reversible, irreversible, precipitate, reaction. Common misconceptions: thinking all color changes are chemical and confusing dissolving (physical) with a chemical reaction.
