Vocabulary: organelle, cell wall, chloroplast, vacuole, mitochondria, nucleus, endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi body, ribosome, structure, function. Common misconceptions: thinking all cells have the same organelles and confusing structure with function. Practice should include comparison activities appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Organisms and environments.skill: Cell organelles: Identify functions of plant cell parts
Science - Organisms and environments.teks_description: Analyze complementary relationship between structure and function
Science - Organisms and environments.prompt: This topic covers the functions of plant cell parts for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.5(F)). Students match each plant cell organelle to its function. Key organelles and functions: cell wall (rigid support and protection), cell membrane (controls what enters/exits), nucleus (contains DNA, controls cell activities), chloroplast (photosynthesis), large central vacuole (water storage, turgor pressure), mitochondria (cellular respiration), ER (protein/lipid production), Golgi (packaging), ribosomes (protein synthesis). Vocabulary: cell wall, cell membrane, nucleus, chloroplast, vacuole, mitochondria, endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi body, ribosome, function. Common misconceptions: confusing cell wall with cell membrane and thinking chloroplasts are in all cells. Practice should include function matching appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Organisms and environments.skill: Cell organelles: Identify functions of animal cell parts
Science - Organisms and environments.teks_description: Analyze complementary relationship between structure and function
Science - Organisms and environments.prompt: This topic covers the functions of animal cell parts for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.5(F)). Students match each animal cell organelle to its function. Key organelles and functions: cell membrane (controls entry/exit), nucleus (contains DNA), mitochondria (cellular respiration/energy), ER (protein/lipid production), Golgi body (packaging and transport), ribosomes (protein synthesis), lysosomes (digestion of waste), centrioles (cell division), cytoplasm (gel-like medium). Vocabulary: cell membrane, nucleus, mitochondria, lysosome, centriole, endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi body, ribosome, cytoplasm, function. Common misconceptions: confusing organelle functions and thinking animal cells have cell walls. Practice should include function matching appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Organisms and environments.skill: Cell organelles: Plant cell diagrams - label parts
Science - Organisms and environments.teks_description: Develop and use models to represent phenomena and systems
Science - Organisms and environments.prompt: This topic covers labeling plant cell diagrams for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.1(G)). Students identify and label the key structures of a plant cell. Key structures include: cell wall, cell membrane, nucleus, chloroplast, large central vacuole, mitochondria, endoplasmic reticulum (smooth and rough), Golgi body, ribosomes, and cytoplasm. Key concepts include: understanding each organelle's function, recognizing structures unique to plant cells (cell wall, chloroplast, large vacuole), and comparing with animal cells. Vocabulary: cell wall, cell membrane, nucleus, chloroplast, vacuole, mitochondria, endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi body, ribosome, cytoplasm. Common misconceptions: confusing cell wall with cell membrane and thinking animal cells have chloroplasts. Practice should include labeling and function matching appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Organisms and environments.skill: Cell organelles: Animal cell diagrams - label parts
Science - Organisms and environments.teks_description: Develop and use models to represent phenomena and systems
Science - Organisms and environments.prompt: This topic covers labeling animal cell diagrams for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.1(G)). Students identify and label the key structures of an animal cell. Key structures include: cell membrane, nucleus, mitochondria, endoplasmic reticulum (smooth and rough), Golgi body, ribosomes, lysosomes, cytoplasm, and centrioles. Key concepts include: understanding each organelle's function, recognizing structures unique to animal cells (centrioles, lysosomes, no cell wall), and comparing with plant cells. Vocabulary: cell membrane, nucleus, mitochondria, lysosome, centriole, endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi body, ribosome, cytoplasm. Common misconceptions: thinking animal cells have cell walls and confusing organelle functions. Practice should include labeling activities appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Organisms and environments.skill: Cell organelles: Compare cells and cell parts
Science - Organisms and environments.teks_description: Analyze complementary relationship between structure and function
Science - Organisms and environments.prompt: This topic covers comparing cells and cell parts for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.5(F)). Students identify similarities and differences between plant and animal cells. Key concepts include: both have nucleus, mitochondria, ER, Golgi, ribosomes, and cell membrane; only plant cells have cell wall, chloroplasts, and large central vacuole; only animal cells have centrioles and lysosomes. The relationship between structure and function explains why different cell types have different organelles. Vocabulary: plant cell, animal cell, organelle, compare, contrast, structure, function, cell wall, chloroplast, centriole. Common misconceptions: thinking plant and animal cells are completely different and not understanding why differences exist. Practice should include Venn diagram comparisons appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Organisms and environments.skill: Cell theory: Recognize the components of cell theory
Science - Organisms and environments.teks_description: Describe hierarchical organization of cells, tissues, organs, organ systems
Science - Organisms and environments.prompt: This topic covers the components of cell theory for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.13(B)). Students learn the three main tenets of cell theory. Key concepts include: (1) all living things are made of one or more cells, (2) cells are the basic unit of structure and function in living things, and (3) all cells come from existing cells. Students should understand the historical development of cell theory (Hooke, Schleiden, Schwann, Virchow) and how microscope technology enabled these discoveries. Vocabulary: cell theory, cell, organism, microscope, Hooke, Schleiden, Schwann, Virchow, basic unit, structure, function. Common misconceptions: thinking viruses disprove cell theory and that cells can arise spontaneously. Practice should include identifying and applying cell theory principles appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Organisms and environments.skill: Cell theory: Understanding cells
Science - Organisms and environments.teks_description: Describe hierarchical organization of cells, tissues, organs, organ systems
Science - Organisms and environments.prompt: This topic covers understanding cells as the fundamental unit of life for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.13(B)). Students develop a comprehensive understanding of cell structure, function, and organization. Key concepts include: cells as the smallest unit of life, prokaryotic vs. eukaryotic cells, unicellular vs. multicellular organisms, cell specialization, and how cells maintain homeostasis. Vocabulary: cell, prokaryotic, eukaryotic, unicellular, multicellular, specialization, homeostasis, organelle, membrane. Common misconceptions: thinking all cells look the same, confusing prokaryotic and eukaryotic, and not understanding cell specialization. Practice should include cell concept exploration appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Organisms and environments.skill: Heredity: Define heredity as the passage of genetic instructions from one generation to the next generation
Science - Organisms and environments.teks_description: Compare results of asexual and sexual reproduction
Science - Organisms and environments.prompt: This topic covers defining heredity for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.13(C)). Students understand that heredity is the passing of genetic information from parents to offspring. Key concepts include: DNA contains genetic instructions, genes are segments of DNA that code for traits, heredity explains why offspring resemble parents, and both sexual and asexual reproduction transmit genetic information. Vocabulary: heredity, genetics, DNA, gene, trait, offspring, parent, inherit, genetic information, chromosome. Common misconceptions: thinking heredity only applies to physical appearance and that all traits are inherited equally from both parents. Practice should include heredity concept exploration appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Organisms and environments.skill: Heredity: Genetic variation in sexual reproduction
Science - Organisms and environments.teks_description: Compare results of asexual and sexual reproduction
Science - Organisms and environments.prompt: This topic covers genetic variation in sexual reproduction for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.13(C)). Students understand how sexual reproduction produces genetically diverse offspring. Key concepts include: meiosis produces gametes with half the chromosomes, fertilization combines genetic material from two parents, crossing over and independent assortment increase variation, and why siblings are genetically different despite having the same parents. Vocabulary: sexual reproduction, genetic variation, meiosis, gamete, fertilization, crossing over, independent assortment, chromosome, allele. Common misconceptions: thinking siblings should be genetically identical and that all variation comes from mutations. Practice should include variation analysis appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Organisms and environments.skill: Heredity: Genes, proteins, and traits - understanding the genetic code
Science - Organisms and environments.teks_description: Compare results of asexual and sexual reproduction
Science - Organisms and environments.prompt: This topic covers the relationship between genes, proteins, and traits for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.13(C)). Students understand the genetic code and how genes determine traits through protein production. Key concepts include: DNA contains the genetic code, genes code for specific proteins, proteins determine physical and chemical traits, mutations in genes can change proteins and thus traits, and the central dogma (DNA -> RNA -> protein). Vocabulary: gene, protein, trait, DNA, genetic code, mutation, amino acid, central dogma, expression. Common misconceptions: thinking one gene controls one trait (many traits involve multiple genes), and confusing genotype with phenotype. Practice should include gene-protein-trait connections appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Organisms and environments.skill: Reproduction: Compare the results of uniform or diverse offspring from asexual or sexual reproduction
Science - Organisms and environments.teks_description: Compare results of asexual and sexual reproduction
Science - Organisms and environments.prompt: This topic covers comparing offspring from asexual and sexual reproduction for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.13(C)). Students compare the genetic outcomes of both reproduction types. Key concepts include: asexual reproduction produces genetically identical offspring (clones), sexual reproduction produces genetically diverse offspring, diversity provides advantages in changing environments, uniformity can be advantageous in stable environments. Vocabulary: asexual reproduction, sexual reproduction, clone, genetic diversity, uniform, offspring, advantage, adaptation. Common misconceptions: thinking asexual reproduction is inferior and that sexual reproduction always produces very different offspring. Practice should include comparison activities appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Organisms and environments.skill: Reproduction: Cell division
Science - Organisms and environments.teks_description: Compare results of asexual and sexual reproduction
Science - Organisms and environments.prompt: This topic covers cell division for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.13(C)). Students understand the basic process of cell division (mitosis) and its role in growth and reproduction. Key concepts include: cells divide to grow, repair, and reproduce, mitosis produces two identical daughter cells, the cell cycle includes interphase and mitosis phases, DNA replication occurs before division, and cell division is essential for life. Vocabulary: cell division, mitosis, cell cycle, interphase, chromosome, DNA replication, daughter cell, growth, repair. Common misconceptions: confusing mitosis with meiosis, thinking cell division creates different cells, and not understanding DNA replication must occur first. Practice should include cell division process analysis appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Organisms and environments.skill: Reproduction: Genetics vocabulary - genotype and phenotype
Science - Organisms and environments.teks_description: Describe how natural and artificial selection change traits in population
Science - Organisms and environments.prompt: This topic covers genotype and phenotype vocabulary for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.13(D)). Students distinguish between genotype (genetic makeup) and phenotype (physical expression). Key concepts include: genotype is the combination of alleles (e.g., Bb), phenotype is the observable trait (e.g., brown eyes), the same phenotype can result from different genotypes, and environment can influence phenotype. Vocabulary: genotype, phenotype, allele, dominant, recessive, homozygous, heterozygous, gene, trait, expression. Common misconceptions: confusing genotype with phenotype, thinking phenotype is solely determined by genotype (environment matters too), and not understanding that different genotypes can produce the same phenotype. Practice should include classification exercises appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Organisms and environments.skill: Reproduction: Genetics vocabulary - dominant and recessive
Science - Organisms and environments.teks_description: Compare results of asexual and sexual reproduction
Science - Organisms and environments.prompt: This topic covers dominant and recessive trait vocabulary for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.13(C)). Students understand how dominant and recessive alleles interact to determine traits. Key concepts include: dominant alleles mask recessive alleles in heterozygous organisms, two recessive alleles are needed for recessive trait expression, using uppercase (B) for dominant and lowercase (b) for recessive, and examples of dominant/recessive traits. Vocabulary: dominant, recessive, allele, homozygous, heterozygous, genotype, phenotype, mask, express, trait. Common misconceptions: thinking dominant means more common, that recessive traits are bad, and that dominant alleles are always expressed (incomplete/codominance). Practice should include allele interaction exercises appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Organisms and environments.skill: Reproduction: Complete and interpret Punnett squares
Science - Organisms and environments.teks_description: Compare results of asexual and sexual reproduction
Science - Organisms and environments.prompt: This topic covers completing and interpreting Punnett squares for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.13(C)). Students use Punnett squares to predict offspring genotypes and phenotypes. Key concepts include: setting up the square with parent alleles, filling in offspring genotype combinations, determining phenotype ratios, and interpreting results. Vocabulary: Punnett square, genotype, phenotype, allele, dominant, recessive, cross, offspring, ratio, predict. Common misconceptions: thinking Punnett squares predict exact outcomes (they show probabilities), placing alleles incorrectly, and confusing genotype ratios with phenotype ratios. Practice should include completing and interpreting squares appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Organisms and environments.skill: Reproduction: Use Punnett squares to calculate ratios of offspring types
Science - Organisms and environments.teks_description: Compare results of asexual and sexual reproduction
Science - Organisms and environments.prompt: This topic covers using Punnett squares to calculate offspring ratios for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.13(C)). Students determine genotypic and phenotypic ratios from Punnett square results. Key concepts include: counting each genotype combination, expressing results as ratios (e.g., 1:2:1 genotypic, 3:1 phenotypic for Bb x Bb), simplifying ratios, and connecting ratios to probability. Vocabulary: Punnett square, ratio, genotypic ratio, phenotypic ratio, offspring, probability, cross, dominant, recessive. Common misconceptions: confusing genotypic and phenotypic ratios, not simplifying ratios correctly, and thinking ratios are exact predictions. Practice should include ratio calculation appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Organisms and environments.skill: Reproduction: Use Punnett squares to calculate probabilities of offspring types
Science - Organisms and environments.teks_description: Compare results of asexual and sexual reproduction
Science - Organisms and environments.prompt: This topic covers using Punnett squares to calculate offspring probabilities for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.13(C)). Students express Punnett square results as probabilities (fractions, decimals, or percentages). Key concepts include: probability = favorable outcomes / total outcomes, converting ratios to probabilities, understanding that each offspring is an independent event, and applying to real-world genetics problems. Vocabulary: probability, Punnett square, offspring, favorable outcome, fraction, percent, independent event, genetic cross. Common misconceptions: thinking probabilities guarantee outcomes, not understanding each offspring is independent, and errors converting between fractions and percentages. Practice should include probability calculation appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Organisms and environments.skill: Reproduction: Angiosperm and conifer life cycles
Science - Organisms and environments.teks_description: Compare results of asexual and sexual reproduction
Science - Organisms and environments.prompt: This topic covers angiosperm and conifer life cycles for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.13(C)). Students compare reproductive strategies of flowering plants (angiosperms) and cone-bearing plants (conifers). Key concepts include: alternation of generations, angiosperms produce flowers and fruits, conifers produce cones, pollination and fertilization processes, seed development, and the role of seeds in dispersal. Vocabulary: angiosperm, conifer, gymnosperm, flower, cone, pollen, seed, fruit, pollination, fertilization, life cycle, alternation of generations. Common misconceptions: thinking all plants have flowers, confusing pollination with fertilization, and not understanding the advantage of seeds over spores. Practice should include life cycle comparison appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Organisms and environments.skill: Reproduction: Moss and fern life cycles
Science - Organisms and environments.teks_description: Compare results of asexual and sexual reproduction
Science - Organisms and environments.prompt: This topic covers moss and fern life cycles for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.13(C)). Students understand reproductive strategies of non-seed plants. Key concepts include: mosses and ferns reproduce using spores (not seeds), alternation of generations with distinct gametophyte and sporophyte stages, requirement for water in reproduction, mosses lack true vascular tissue while ferns have it, and evolutionary significance as early land plants. Vocabulary: moss, fern, spore, gametophyte, sporophyte, alternation of generations, vascular tissue, non-vascular, frond, rhizome. Common misconceptions: thinking mosses and ferns use seeds, confusing gametophyte and sporophyte stages, and not understanding why water is required. Practice should include life cycle analysis appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Organisms and environments.skill: Genes and chromosomes: Recognize that inherited traits of individuals are governed in the genetic material found in the genes within chromosomes in the nucleus
Science - Organisms and environments.teks_description: Compare results of asexual and sexual reproduction
Science - Organisms and environments.prompt: This topic covers how inherited traits are governed by genetic material for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.13(C)). Students understand that traits are controlled by genes located on chromosomes within the cell nucleus. Key concepts include: DNA is organized into chromosomes in the nucleus, genes are specific segments of DNA, genes carry instructions for traits, alleles are different versions of the same gene, and how genotype determines phenotype. Vocabulary: inherited trait, gene, chromosome, nucleus, DNA, allele, genotype, phenotype, genetic material. Common misconceptions: thinking traits are determined by a single gene and that genetic material is in the cytoplasm. Practice should include gene-trait connections appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Organisms and environments.skill: Genes and chromosomes: Genes, proteins, and traits - understanding the genetic code
Science - Organisms and environments.teks_description: Compare results of asexual and sexual reproduction
Science - Organisms and environments.prompt: This topic covers the relationship between genes, proteins, and traits for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.13(C)). Students understand the genetic code and how genes determine traits through protein production. Key concepts include: DNA contains the genetic code, genes code for specific proteins, proteins determine physical and chemical traits, mutations in genes can change proteins and thus traits, and the central dogma (DNA -> RNA -> protein). Vocabulary: gene, protein, trait, DNA, genetic code, mutation, amino acid, central dogma, expression. Common misconceptions: thinking one gene controls one trait (many traits involve multiple genes), and confusing genotype with phenotype. Practice should include gene-protein-trait connections appropriate for 12-13 year olds.

Science - Organisms and environments.skill: Genes and chromosomes: Describe the effects of gene mutations on organisms
Science - Organisms and environments.teks_description: Describe how natural and artificial selection change traits in population
Science - Organisms and environments.prompt: This topic covers the effects of gene mutations on organisms for Grade 7 science students (TEKS 7.13(D)). Students understand how changes in DNA can affect organisms. Key concepts include: mutations are changes in DNA sequence, some mutations are harmful (genetic diseases), some are neutral (no effect), some are beneficial (advantageous traits), mutations provide raw material for natural selection, and environmental mutagens can cause mutations. Vocabulary: mutation, gene, DNA, beneficial, harmful, neutral, mutagen, genetic disease, variation, natural selection. Common misconceptions: thinking all mutations are harmful, that mutations always produce visible changes, and that organisms can direct mutations. Practice should include mutation analysis appropriate for 12-13 year olds.
